topic: Whole numbers: place value, representing and ordering 6-digit numbers
learning_objectives: Revise place value of digits in whole numbers up to 6 digits (hundred thousands to ones).
Read, write and represent 6-digit numbers in standard form, expanded notation and number words.
Order and compare 6-digit numbers using place value reasoning and symbols (<, =, >).
Identify the value of a given digit in a 6-digit number.
prior_knowledge: Counting, ordering, comparing and representing 4- to 5-digit whole numbers (Grade 5).
Place value of digits in numbers up to 99 999.
Expanded notation for numbers up to 5 digits.
Using number lines, number grids and structured diagrams to represent numbers.
Odd and even numbers to at least 1 000.

lesson_flow.activity: Mental mathematics warm-up: learners count forwards and backwards in 10s, 100s and 1 000s from varied starting points (not always multiples). Quick oral revision — state the value of the underlined digit in 4- and 5-digit numbers (e.g. 47 382; 128 905). Pair-share: one learner says a 5-digit number, partner states how many ten thousands, thousands, hundreds, tens and units it has.
lesson_flow.teacher_notes: CAPS recommends 10 minutes of mental mathematics daily. Keep number ranges at Grade 5 level for this starter; increase range only after learners show confidence. Use a number line or number grid on the board if learners need visual support.

lesson_flow.activity: Introduce the 6-digit number range (0 to 999 999). Display a place value chart to hundred thousands. Model reading and writing numbers such as 384 726 and 605 091, including numbers with internal zeros. Demonstrate expanded notation (e.g. 384 726 = 300 000 + 80 000 + 4 000 + 700 + 20 + 6). Use Dienes blocks or place value cards to build one number physically, then write it in three forms: digits, words and expanded notation.
lesson_flow.teacher_notes: CAPS Term 1 allocates 2 hours to place value for 6-digit numbers across the term; Day 1 establishes the foundation. Emphasise that each place is ten times the place to its right. Link explicitly to Grade 5 work: 'We already know thousands — today we extend to hundred thousands.' Avoid introducing 7- to 9-digit numbers today.

lesson_flow.activity: Whole-class and pair work: (1) Given 6-digit numbers on place value cards, learners arrange digits to make the largest and smallest possible number. (2) Compare pairs using <, = or > — e.g. 247 889 ___ 247 898; 395 431 ___ 395 413. (3) Place given numbers on a semi-structured number line (e.g. mark the number halfway between 471 340 and 471 350). (4) Fill in missing numbers in a sequence increasing by 1 000 or 10 000.
lesson_flow.teacher_notes: Compare digit by digit from left to right (hundred thousands first). When digits match, move to the next place. Use questioning: 'Which place value is different first?' Correct common errors with internal zeros (e.g. 605 091 vs 650 091).

lesson_flow.activity: Learners complete a worksheet with four sections: (A) Write 6-digit numbers from number words; (B) Write expanded notation for given numbers; (C) Order a set of six 6-digit numbers from smallest to largest; (D) Circle the greater number in each pair and justify in one sentence using place value.
lesson_flow.teacher_notes: Circulate and check that learners use place value language in justifications, not just 'because it looks bigger.' Support learners who struggle by giving them place value charts; extension learners may write two different 6-digit numbers that share the same thousands digit but differ in ten thousands.

lesson_flow.activity: Exit ticket: (1) Write 562 408 in expanded notation. (2) Compare 784 109 and 785 190 using <, = or >. (3) One learner explains aloud how they knew which number was greater. Brief preview: tomorrow we extend representing and comparing to larger numbers and revisit counting in larger intervals.
lesson_flow.teacher_notes: Collect exit tickets to inform Day 2 grouping. Reinforce that all work this week builds toward ordering, comparing and representing numbers up to 9 digits (ATP Week 1), but today focused deliberately on secure 6-digit understanding per CAPS Term 1 pacing.

resources: Place value chart (ones to hundred thousands)
Place value cards or flash cards

formative: Observe pair-share responses during the starter.
Monitor guided practice comparisons and number-line placement.
Mark exit ticket: expanded notation accuracy and correct use of <, =, >.
Listen for place value vocabulary during the plenary explanation.
success_criteria: I can state the value of any digit in a 6-digit number.
I can write a 6-digit number in standard form, words and expanded notation.
I can order at least four 6-digit numbers from smallest to largest.
I can compare two 6-digit numbers using <, = or > and explain my reasoning.

support: Provide a laminated place value chart for reference throughout the lesson.
Start comparisons with numbers that differ only in one place value column.
Use physical Dienes blocks before asking learners to write expanded notation.
Reduce worksheet to three items per section; allow oral responses where writing is a barrier.
extension: Find all 6-digit numbers that can be made from the digits 3, 8, 4, 7, 2, 6 where no digit repeats; order them.
Write a 6-digit number where the sum of the digits is 30; explain your strategy.
Pose a number riddle: 'My hundred thousands digit is twice my tens digit; my thousands digit is 0…' for a partner to solve.

homework: Write five 6-digit numbers of your own. For each: (a) write the number in words, (b) write expanded notation, (c) circle the digit in the ten thousands place. Order your five numbers from smallest to largest.

caps_alignment.atp_week_focus: Week 1: Revision of Grade 5 work (integrated); whole numbers — counting, ordering, comparing, representing and place value (6–9-digit numbers). Day 1 addresses the CAPS Term 1 entry point of 6-digit place value, representing and ordering before extending to 9-digit numbers, prime numbers and rounding later in the week.
